rulings.Case Title: KRISTINA NENAYDOKH VS. GREG SHENKMAN et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For An Order Compelling Judgment Debtor Greg Shenkman To Provide Responses To Post-Judgment Written Discovery And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, February 27, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFF KRISTINA NENAYDOKH'S Motion For An Order Compelling Judgment Debtor Greg Shenkman To Provide Responses To Post-Judgment Written Discovery And For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Tony Schoenberg, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Continued to March 6, 2019. JPT Schoenberg disqualifies himself. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to tschoenberg@fbm.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: HOEM & ASSOCIATES INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. UNIVERSITY OF THE PACIFIC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Of Maxxon Corporations And Armstrong Flooring, Incs Cross Complaints
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, February 27, 2019, Line 1, CROSS-DEFENDANT NOVA PARTNERS, INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Of Maxxon Corporations and Armstrong Flooring, Inc.'s Cross-Complaints. Cross-defendant Nova Partners, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is denied. The motion is denied as moot as to the cross-complaint by Armstrong Flooring, Inc. because that cross-complaint was dismissed on January 10, 2019. Nova's motion is denied as to the Maxxon Corporation cross-complaint because Nova failed to maintain its burden of production. The Court sustains Maxxon's objection to the Marks declaration because he failed to sign it. (Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 2015.5; In re Marriage of Reese & Guy (1999) 73 Cal.App.4th 1214, 1222-1223 [attorney's "custom of signing declarations under penalty of perjury on behalf of her clients and witnesses . . . [was] completely improper" and would warrant a future award of sanctions.].) Because that declaration is excluded, the consulting agreement between Nova and the University of the Pacific ("UOP")-which Nova failed timely to file with its moving papers-is not properly before the Court. In addition, Nova's motion fails on the merits. Nova fails to establish that there is no basis for liability. Page 9 of the Nova/UOP consulting agreement and Mr. Jordan's testimony show that Nova had a more active role on the project than it contends and could be comparatively at fault for the flooring problems. (Jordan Depo., 42:15-43:12; 59:14-17; 61:23-63:7.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Maxxon is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: SAN FRANCISCO DEPUTY SHERIFFS' ASSOCIATION, ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Defendant City And County Of San Franciscos Motion For An Order To Requiring Plaintiff John Doe To Proceed Under True Name
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, February 27, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S Motion For An Order Requiring Plaintiff "John Doe" To Proceed Under True Name. Defendant City and County of San Francisco's motion for an order requiring Plaintiff "John Doe" to proceed under his true name is granted. While there is no blanket rule against the use of fictitious names by plaintiffs in cases where (as here) such a procedure is not explicitly authorized by statute, a plaintiff may not use a fictitious name except when unusual circumstances justify protecting his true identity, such as when identification creates a risk of retaliatory physical or mental harm, when anonymity is necessary to preserve the plaintiff's privacy in a matter of sensitive and highly personal nature, or when the anonymous party is compelled to admit his intention to engage in illegal conduct, thereby risking criminal prosecution. (Doe v. Lincoln Unified School Dist. (2010) 188 Cal.App.4th 758, 767, citing Does I thru XXIII v. Advanced Textile Corp. (9th Cir. 2000) 214 F.3d 1058, 1067 [dicta]; see, e.g., Doe v. Superior Court (Luster) (2011) 194 Cal.App.4th 750, 754 [rape victim permitted to file complaint under fictitious name in order to preserve her anonymity].) Here, Plaintiff has not shown that the disclosure of his identity would jeopardize his physical safety, subject him to retaliation, or otherwise cause an unwarranted invasion of privacy that would outweigh the public right of access to court records. He contends that his termination was previously the subject of television news stories that appear to have relied on information from his confidential disciplinary personnel files that he believes were illegally leaked to the news outlet, but he has not shown that as a result of such news coverage, he was previously subjected to any retaliation or threats to his physical safety. Further, the prior disclosure of Doe's identity and other personal information undermines his current claim of a compelling privacy interest in such information that would justify him in proceeding anonymously. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: YUSON WEI YUE LUI VS. ANNA YIT NOR LUI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Anna Lui, Lily Lui, Raymond Lui, Ng Lui Notice Of Motion To Quash (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only, Part 2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, February 27, 2019, Line 4, Defendants Anna Lui, Lily Lui, Raymond Lui, Ng Lui Notice Of Motion To Quash (For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only, Part 2 Of 2) This is not necessarily a reasonable assumption. First, it does not necessarily follow that because the Wells Fargo account was only used for the apartment's rents and expenses, every deposit into the account constituted "missing rental income." Standing alone, the single deposit on one occasion in 2017 from a Chase bank account does not necessarily lead to any particular conclusion about the Chase account. Here, Defendants have provided an explanation for the Chase deposit - that is, it was money that was being distributed to the family members from the Chase account when it was closed after the father's death, and Defendants have provided photocopies of distribution checks that corroborate this story. Plaintiffs have questioned this explanation. But even if the Chase explanation were incorrect, it does not necessarily follow that the Chase account was used as a repository for absconded rental funds. Plaintiffs already have obtained discovery of bank records of the four bank accounts that were actually used for the rental property; tax returns for the years in which the parties co-owned the property; and spreadsheets showing the amount of rent and expense from the rental business. If there was, as alleged, a breach of an oral agreement to share profits from the rental property business, that should be demonstrated by the documents that plaintiff has discovered. And even assuming that more discovery were warranted about the single payment from the Chase account in April 2017, it can be obtained through more narrowly tailored discovery than the broad subpoenas that were served on Chase. For the foregoing reasons, the information sought in the Chase subpoena is not "essential to determining the truth" of the matters in dispute in this case. Plaintiffs also argue that the motion should be denied because it was not accompanied by a separate statement. But a separate statement is not required as part of a motion to quash a subpoena for the production of business records other than at a deposition. See CRC 3.1345(a). The subpoenas at issue on this motion do not seek the production of documents at a deposition. Defendants request judicial notice of Plaintiffs' complaint and an earlier filed motion to quash in this matter. The request is granted only as to the fact of the filing of the two documents, but not as to any of the contents of the two documents. Plaintiffs' request for sanctions is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to tschoenberg@fbm.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of tentative ruling part 2 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: YUSON WEI YUE LUI VS. ANNA YIT NOR LUI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Quash
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, February 27, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT ANNA LUI, LILY LUI, RAYMOND LUI, NG LUI Notice Of Motion To Quash (part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge Tony Schoenberg, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is granted. Plaintiffs do not dispute that the J. P. Morgan Chase ("Chase") bank account records sought in the subpoenas are protected by the right to privacy, and Plaintiffs concede that such materials are discoverable only if they are "directly relevant to a cause of action or defense, i.e., that it is essential to determining the truth of the matters in dispute. See Harris v. Superior Court, 3 Cal. App. 4th 661, 665 (1992). The subpoenas to Chase do not seek information that is "essential to determining the truth of the matters in dispute" in this case. The gravamen of Plaintiffs' complaint is that Defendant Toy orally agreed to manage the family's apartment rental business at 1675 Clay Street - and to distribute the profits of the business among the owners, which consist of other family members - in exchange for being permitted to occupy one unit of the building rent-free. Plaintiffs contend that Defendant Toy has failed to distribute the profits to them and has shared the profits with the other defendants. Plaintiffs' sole justification for seeking discovery from the Chase bank account is that on one occasion in late April 2017, Defendant Toy deposited a cashier's check in the amount of $30,925.64 into a Wells Fargo account that was used to handle funds for the rental property. From that one fact, Plaintiffs leap to the conclusion that "since Defendants claim that Wells Fargo Bank account was only used for the apartment's rents and expenses, the reasonable assumption is that the money from Chase was part of the missing rental income." (Opp. at p. 5) (end of tentative ruling of part 1 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: HAROLD H. ROBINSON IV VS. JENNIFER M. JOHNSON ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Deposition And Document Production
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, February 27, 2019, Line 6, PLAINTIFF HAROLD ROBINSON Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Deposition And Document Production (part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge Tony Schoenberg, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is granted. As to the PMQ deposition of SBH, CCP Section 2025.010 allows Robinson to take the deposition of any person, which is defined to include an entity. Thus, Robinson is entitled to take the deposition of SBH, which is both a "person" and a party to this action. Section 2025.610, on which SBH relies to support its position, specifically permits a party to take the deposition of a natural person after that person has testified as a PMQ of an entity. Accordingly, it does not support SBH's position. The "good cause" requirement that SBH invokes only applies where a party seeks to take the deposition of a natural person who has previously been deposed individually; it does not apply where a natural person is deposed after testifying as a PMQ. Moreover, SBH's invocation of the Rifkind case is unavailing, as that case only prohibits deposition questions about legal contentions and specifically permits deposition questions about the basis for factual assertions. See Rifkind v. Superior Court, 22 Cal. App. 4th 1255, 1259 (1994). A number of the categories in the deposition notice concern factual assertions rather than legal contentions, and SBH implicitly concedes this, as it agreed to produce a witness in response to many of the categories in the notice. (end of tentative ruling part 1 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: HAROLD H. ROBINSON IV VS. JENNIFER M. JOHNSON ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Harold Robinson Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Deposition And Document Production (Tentative Ruling Purposes Only Part 1 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, February 27, 2019, Line 6, Plaintiff Harold Robinson Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Deposition And Document Production (Tentative Ruling Purposes Only Part 2 Of 2) This ruling is without prejudice to a later motion in the event that Robinson seeks to depose Jennifer Johnson or Cyrus Johnson as individuals (though this is not intended to suggest any inclination to grant or deny such a motion, should it ever be filed). The deposition of SBH should take place within 10 days of the date of this Order, of any later date to which the parties agree. As to the requests for production to SBH, SBI, Chung and Devereaux, each of the foregoing parties and third parties must complete production of the documents that they have agreed to produce no later than March 15, 2019. Each of the foregoing parties and third parties served objections and responses on September 10, 2018, in which they agreed to produce documents responsive to many categories of the requests. As of the date of this ruling, it has been more than five months since those responses were served, and it will be more than half a year by the March 15 production date. That is more than ample time to complete a document production, even in a case involving large volumes of documents. Given the representation that documents have already been produced or made available for inspection constituting "a significant quantity of responsive, non-privileged documents of which SBH, SBI and Johnson are aware," this should not impose an undue burden. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to tschoenberg@fbm.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (end of part 2 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: HAROLD H. ROBINSON IV VS. JENNIFER M. JOHNSON ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, February 27, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANT JENNIFER JOHNSON Motion For Protective Order. Pro Tem Judge Tony Schoenberg, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is denied. CCP Section 2025.010 allows Robinson to take the deposition of any person, which is defined to include an entity. Thus, Robinson is entitled to take the deposition of SBH, which is both a "person" and a party to this action. Section 2025.610, on which SBH relies to support its motion, specifically permits a party to take the deposition of a natural person after that person has testified as a PMQ of an entity. Accordingly, it does not support SBH's position. The "good cause" requirement that SBH invokes only applies where a party seeks to take the deposition of a natural person who has previously been deposed individually; it does not apply where a natural person is deposed after testifying as a PMQ. Moreover, SBH's invocation of the Rifkind case is unavailing, as that case only prohibits deposition questions about legal contentions and specifically permits deposition questions about the basis for factual assertions. See Rifkind v. Superior Court, 22 Cal. App. 4th 1255, 1259 (1994). A number of the categories in the deposition notice concern factual assertions rather than legal contentions, and SBH implicitly concedes this, as it agreed to produce a witness in response to many of the categories in the notice. This ruling is without prejudice to a later motion in the event that Robinson seeks to depose Jennifer Johnson or Cyrus Johnson as individuals (though this is not intended to suggest any inclination to grant or deny such a motion, should it ever be filed). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to tschoenberg@fbm.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ***TRANSFERRED TO CONTRA COSTA COUNTY*** OBAIDULLAH ALI VS. SCOTT HELLER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR CHANGE OF VENUE
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, February 27, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANTS SCOTT HELLER AND HELLER JEWELERS, INC.'S MOTION FOR CHANGE OF VENUE. Defendants Scott Heller and Heller Jewelers, Inc.'s motion to transfer venue of this action to the Superior Court of California, County of Contra Costa is granted. The proper court for the trial of this action is Contra Costa County, the residence of both Defendants, and the county in which the alleged contract was entered into, was to be performed, and was allegedly breached. (Code Civ. Proc. Secs. 395(a), 395.5.) A cause of action for defamation or slander is not an action "for injury to person" within the meaning of Section 395(a). (Graham v. Mixon (1917) 177 Cal. 88, 93; see also Carruth v. Superior Court (1978) 80 Cal.App.3d 215, 217-218 [action for malicious prosecution is not one "for injury to person" as provided under section 395, and fact that plaintiff sought emotional distress damages did not alter that conclusion].) Within five days, Plaintiff's counsel Anthony A. Ferrigno shall pay Defendants' reasonable expenses in bringing the motion in the amount of $60, plus the processing change of venue fee of $40 and $435 for the initial filing fee. (Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 396b(b).) The Court finds that an offer to stipulate to change of venue was reasonably made and rejected, and that Plaintiff's counsel did not act in good faith in selecting the venue originally or in opposing the motion. Defendants' request for an award of reasonable attorneys' fees is denied in light of their failure to support that request with a proper declaration showing the number of hours spent preparing the motion or the reasonable hourly rates charged by counsel. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Maxxon is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: WILLIAM Y MOORES MD VS. MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA A GOVERNMENTAL ENTITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, February 27, 2019, Line 7, RESPONDENTS MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA, KIMBERLY KIRCHMEYER, AND DEV GNANADEV, M.D.'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED PETITION (PART 1 OF 2 FOR PURPOSES OF ENTRY OF TENTATIVE RULING ONLY). Respondents Medical Board of California, Kimberley Kirchmeyer, and Dev Gnanadev, M.D.'s demurrer to Petitioner William Y. Moores, M.D.'s first amended petition for writ of mandate and complaint for damages and declaratory and injunctive relief is sustained without leave to amend as to all nine causes of action. Petitioner's claims fail for several reasons-most notably, expiration of statutory limitations periods and principles of res judicata. Per Evidence Code sections 452(c)-(d), and 453, the Court takes judicial notice of the judicial and administrative records presented by Respondents. The Court declines to take judicial notice of the correspondence attached to Petitioner's request for judicial notice, which is neither a proper subject for judicial notice nor relevant to the resolution of the demurrer. As indicated in this Court's previous ruling, affirmed on appeal, administrative mandamus is Petitioner's exclusive remedy for challenging the Board's 2013 decision. (RJN Exh. A, p. 2, Exh. B, p. 13.) However, Petitioner's writ is time-barred because he did not file his writ within 30 days of the decision. Petitioner cannot circumvent the 30-day period set by Government Code section 11523 by framing a writ of mandamus challenging a discretionary act as an action testing the legality of the penalty imposed by the Board. (See Morton v. Board of Registered Nursing (1991) 235 Cal.App.3d 1560, 1564, 1566 [holding that because the action challenged by petitioner was discretionary rather than mandatory, she could not seek relief under CCP section 1085 rather than CCP section 1094.5].) Similarly, Petitioner cannot disguise his mandamus efforts as a breach of contract action under the settlement agreement to avoid the reality that he did not seek the writ within the 30-day period as required. Petitioner's damages claim fails because he did not present a claim to the Board before seeking damages as required by Government Code section 945.4. (See TrafficSchoolOnline v. Clarke (2003) 112 Cal.App.4th 736, 739, 741 ["Government Code section 945.4 explicitly states 'no' damage suit may be pursued unless there is compliance with the claim statutes. . . . 'No' means no."].) No evidence has been presented that any of the exceptions to section 945.4 apply here. Mata v. City of Los Angeles (1993) 20 Cal.App.4th 141, 149 does not compel a different result as that case involved a plaintiff seeking compensatory damages pursuant to 42 U.S.C. Sec.1983 for violation of federal civil rights, not a writ of mandate under CCP section 1095. Furthermore, Petitioner's claims are barred by res judicata as his causes of action merely repackage his old rejected claims under different theories. (See Lincoln Property Co. v. Travelers Indemnity Co. (2006) 137 Cal.App.4th 905, 912 ["Res judicata precludes piecemeal litigation by splitting a single cause of action or re-litigation of the same cause of action on a different legal theory or for different relief."].) In addition, "prior final judgment on the merits not only settles issues that were actually litigated but also every issue that might have been raised and litigated in the first action." (Id. at 913.) Res judicata precludes Petitioner from continuing to litigate the issues stemming from his disagreement with the Board's 2013 decisions to not adopt the ALJ's proposed decision and refuse to terminate his probation. (See Mycogen Corp. v. Monsanto Co. (2002) 28 Cal.4th 888, 897 [holding that res judicata seeks to curtail multiple litigation to conserve the resources of the court and the parties].) (Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: WILLIAM Y MOORES MD VS. MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA A GOVERNMENTAL ENTITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioners Medical Board Of California, Kimberly Kirchmeyer, And Dev Gnanadev, M.D.'S Demurrer To 1st Amended Petition (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, February 27, 2019, Line 7, RESPONDENTS MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA, KIMBERLY KIRCHMEYER, AND DEV GNANADEV, M.D.'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED PETITION (PART 2 OF 2 FOR PURPOSES OF ENTRY OF TENTATIVE RULING ONLY). To the extent that Petitioner seeks to assert claims by way of a writ of mandamus, they are barred by the 30-day limitations period as explained above. (RJN Exh. B, p. 13.) As such, causes of action one through five are barred as repackaged claims seeking judicial review of the same administrative acts. In addition, causes of action six, eight, and nine all relate to the Board's licensing decision. Petitioner was required to raise these claims at the time of the prior lawsuit and such claims are now barred. (See Lincoln Property Co., 137 Cal.App.4th at 913, 916 [holding that demurrer was properly sustained as to claims for damages that arose out of the same alleged violation as other claims but were not pursued in the prior litigation].) The seventh cause of action for breach of a mandatory duty was rejected by the Court of Appeal's holding that there is no mandatory duty to respond to potential employers in the manner alleged by Petitioner. (RJN Exh. B, p. 19.) Petitioner fails to cite authority supporting his assertion that a res judicata bar to a "special proceeding" such as a writ of mandamus requires the issues to have been adjudicated in a prior special proceeding rather than a regular civil suit. City of Oakland v. Oakland Police & Fire Retirement System (2014) 224 Cal.App.4th 210, 229 and Overstreet v. County of Butte (1962) 57 Cal.2d 504, 506 merely hold that a prior writ proceeding will bar a subsequent writ proceeding. Neither case holds that the doctrine of res judicata is only applicable to a writ proceeding if the prior proceeding was also a writ proceeding. While Petitioner is correct that changed circumstances could make claim preclusion inappropriate in certain contexts (see, e.g., Neil Norman, Ltd. v. William Kasper & Co. (1983) 149 Cal.App.3d 942, 947), the only new facts alleged by Petitioner are that he continues to contend that he is engaging in the practice of medicine. Petitioner's ongoing disagreement with the Board's finding that his activities do not constitute the practice of medicine for purposes of his probation is not a new fact that would preclude the application of res judicata. Finally, Government Code sections 818.4 and 821.1 provide immunity to the Board and its employees from claims arising out of licensing decisions. (See Colome v. State Athletic Com. of Cal. (1996) 47 Cal.App.4th 1444, 1456 [holding that immunity is absolute for discretionary licensing decisions by public agencies].) Accordingly, Petitioner's claims for damages are barred for this additional reason. Given the multiple defects from which Petitioner's claims suffer, there is no reasonable possibility that Petitioner could amend to save his lawsuit.
